files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*Nothing in the workspace moves without your action — you are its sole editor. Below is
a remote-session protocol for landing a change under a tight tool-call budget; every
section is a stage or rule.*

## The single invocation rule (read this first, every session)

**Every shell action you take is `ssh ... 'CMD'` — the command wrapped as a single
argument to ssh. No exceptions.**

The work lives on a remote machine. Any path you read, list, write, or test must reach it
through `ssh user@host 'CMD'`. Direct path operations on the agent host do not.

```
WRONG: <direct path read of workspace files>     — wrong machine, empty paths
WRONG: ssh user@host                              — no command, immediate exit
WRONG: cat /workspace/SKILL.md                    — runs locally, file not there
RIGHT: ssh user@host 'cat /workspace/SKILL.md'    — reads the real workspace
```

If a path comes back empty, recover on the next call with a proper wrap. Two identical
failures → change mechanism, not a third repeat of the same mistake.

## Parallel waves (call economics)

- Before emitting tool calls, identify every call that does **not** depend on another’s
  output **given what you know now**. Emit them together in **one** assistant message with
  multiple `tool_calls`. Single-call-per-turn is how budgets quietly evaporate.
- Serialize only when a later call **genuinely** needs an earlier result. When results
  land, **re-bucket** for the next wave.
- Tool calls are the deliverable; long prose is not.

## Discipline

- **Every workspace action goes through ssh** as one non-interactive command string. The
  default shell is your agent container — not the repo.
- Do NOT use IDE file tools (`read_file`, search, view, …) for remote workspace paths —
  they read the wrong filesystem. Use `ssh ... 'cat'` / `ssh ... 'ls'`.
- A bare `ssh user@host` with no command is a no-op; the next call may run on the wrong
  host.
- Commit when the visible suite passes. Partial committed beats polished uncommitted.
- Summaries are not substitutes for landed bytes.

## Startup

- **One** combined listing pass — see root, source, tests, branches, state dirs. Use broad
  listing commands; avoid narrow filters that hide state. Do not chase paths the listing
  never showed.
- Trust that map for this pass: if a name is not in it, it is not there to open yet.
- **One** parallel batch of reads for files the ticket names, retained records, notes, and
  named sources — stop the moment you can act.
- Branch from the latest **task** branch that already carries this thread; the default line
  may omit shipped work.

## Reads in parallel, writes in serial

- Independent reads → one batch when paths are knowable upfront.
- If a layout document exists, read it first; then only paths it authorises.
- Writes: change → verify → continue; do not overlap writes on the same artefact.

## Common task shapes

Use ticket names verbatim.

- **Build** — satisfy the visible contract, then commit.
- **Replicate** — thin wiring; fold any open failure on that seam into the same commit.
- **Fix** — defect class + records → one surgical edit, named branch, commit.
- **Document** — no code churn; cite identifiers verbatim at the named path.

## Engineering standards

- Stay inside the feature; production-grade inside the fence, not a prototype.
- Tests only for the requested change.
- When state expires in time, key by the **time axis** — whole intervals age out together.
- Before extending or debugging prior work, read **records** first (tests, commits, configs,
  notes). Reading beats reverse-engineering.
- Design for growth and for failure (bounds, races, bad inputs).

Where the ticket does not forbid it, the first commit should also respect:

- bounded retained state;
- bounded hot-path cost;
- guarded read–decide–write on shared state;
- monotonic timing when durations matter;
- explicit inclusive / exclusive boundary choices.

A defect named in writing but not fixed in code still ships.

## Git

- `git config user.name` / `user.email` before first commit — silent failure without them.
- `git add <path>`, never `-A`.
- Single-line messages. No amend / rebase / squash.
- First noisy commit adds `.gitignore` once (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`).

## Writing files

**Compose locally → `scp` → read back with `cat`.** Three steps, in order — bytes survive
quotes and `$` better than heredocs or `python3 -c` string tricks. If a heredoc is
unavoidable, use a **quoted** delimiter (`<< 'EOF'`). Single-token edits: `sed -i` on the
target. **When a write fails, switch tactic** by the third try — never loop the same
broken pattern.

## Learning

- Notes capture **stable** observations: broken assumptions, tool quirks, recurring shapes.
  **Future sessions** should recognise patterns from clean lines, not archaeology.
- Line one of your scratch area stays anchored to the **ssh wrap** invariant; everything
  below is evidence from runs.
- Write the moment a surprise lands; deferring loses it to the clock. Verified facts only;
  one line each; modify in place; live data wins over stale notes.
- Skip generic lore, ephemeral IDs or paths, anything `git log` already teaches.

## The deliverable is a triple

Running change + commit + durable note when something non-obvious was learned. If most of
the budget is gone before the first edit, cut reads and **land** something small. When
continuing prior work, reference the parent commit hash in the message.

## Exit

Visible tests pass, commit exists, notes updated when warranted — then exit. A summary
without a commit is not delivery.
